I've changed the minimum-offset-tolerance to be user settable in 606fa79 (in fact it turns out no minimum offset tolerance was being used, so this also caught a bug that made it through the tests).

Regarding `with_offset`, I had it the way you propose before, but I actually prefer to have an explicit argument for this (hence `with_offset`). The `evaluate` method always computes the metrics both with and without offsets. If ignoring offsets is made implicit (by `offset_ratio=None`), then when `offset_ratio=None` the two sets of metrics will be identical, even though one is supposedly with offsets and the other without (unless I add some clunky logic into `evaluate`, which I'd rather not).

By having an explicit argument, this difference is made as clear as possible to the user, and since I expect most users to use the `evaluate` method, I think this avoids potential confusion. Long story short, unless there's serious opposition, I'd rather keep it as is.

@snowch @ricellis I think it's best to move forward without the global variables and construct one client per thread.  The example in the above comment adds confusion and I don't have enough evidence that performance will be all that better.
```
...
   @app.route('/')
   def hello_world():
      # Cookie authentication can be renewed automatically using ``auto_renew=True``
      # which is typically what you would require when running in an application
      # server where the connection may stay open for a long period of time
      client = Cloudant(USERNAME, PASSWORD,
                                    url='https://acct.cloudant.com',
                                    auto_renew=True,
                                    connect=True)

      # do something with client
      return 'Hello World!'
...
```

I'm not familiar with flask, but my reading of this is that each defined `@app.route` is a HTTP handler and they can be serviced on multiple threads. Due to the ambiguity of requests/urllib3 around thread-safe cookie handling we are saying it is unsafe to access a single client object from multiple threads and hence we can't just store a cloudant `client` for use by all the http handlers. Instantiating a `client` on each handler call obviously has a performance penalty.

The alternative solution in my mind is to create a pool of `client` resources and lease one in the http handler? Similar to the way a  JDBC connection pool operates, does flask offer that kind of resource pooling? If it was done that way then the `client`s would be instantiated in the resource pool and used by only 1 HTTP handler thread at a time?

The hypothetical concern here is a race condition where two threads both say `x = exceptions.X` and both factories decide to create a class for it. Now we have two different identities for the exception class X - one canonical, and one orphaned. The thread whose reference got overwritten will fail to catch the resulting exception with `try .. except X`, because it is no longer an instance of X.

It is worth mentioning that allowing dependencies here breaks the original vision for python_dist as a Pants-specific apparatus for depending on a properly-packaged and readily-consumable python distributions (that exists in the python ecosystem with nothing Pants-specific at all). Clearly it is crucial to this PR that we allow dependencies and I don't think it it is a problem, but I wanted to mention that we may be stepping on/adding additional conceptual loading onto what a "python distribution" is in Pants context. Please use the `IS` operator here.

> The IS and IS NOT operators work like = and != except when one or both of the operands are NULL. In this case, if both operands are NULL, then the IS operator evaluates to 1 (true) and the IS NOT operator evaluates to 0 (false). If one operand is NULL and the other is not, then the IS operator evaluates to 0 (false) and the IS NOT operator is 1 (true). It is not possible for an IS or IS NOT expression to evaluate to NULL. Operators IS and IS NOT have the same precedence as =.

If this were my code, I would have probably done it her way, but with a bit more narrative:

```python
def exception(self, timeout=None):
    """Return the exception, if any, or None otherwise."""

    # Sanity check: If there is no current exception, then just return None.
    if not self._poll(timeout).HasField('error'):
        return None

    # Okay, there was an exception as expected; return it.
    return self._operation.error
```
